hints.Iustus: 'Iustus' is an adjective/noun meaning 'the just one' — it is the subject
hints.fide: 'fide' is a noun in the ablative meaning 'faith'

hints.Imperfect: Imperfect of 'vivere' 3rd person would be 'vivebat' — 'vivit' has the present -it ending
hints.Perfect: Perfect of 'vivere' 3rd person would be 'vixit' — 'vivit' is present tense
hints.Future: Future of 'vivere' 3rd person would be 'vivet' — 'vivit' has the present -it ending

hints.vinco, vincere, vici, victum: These are the principal parts of 'vincere' (to conquer)
hints.video, videre, vidi, visum: These are the principal parts of 'videre' (to see)
hints.venio, venire, veni, ventum: These are the principal parts of 'venire' (to come)

hints.Genitive: Genitive of 'iustus' would be 'iusti' — 'Iustus' is the nominative form
hints.Accusative: Accusative of 'iustus' would be 'iustum' — 'Iustus' is the nominative form
hints.Vocative: Vocative of 'iustus' would be 'iuste' — 'Iustus' is the subject here

hints.Nominative: Nominative of 'fides' is 'fides' — 'fide' has the ablative -e ending
hints.Genitive: Genitive of 'fides' would be 'fidei' — 'fide' has the ablative ending
hints.Dative: Dative of 'fides' would be 'fidei' — 'fide' is the ablative form after 'ex'

hints.1st declension: 1st declension nouns have genitive in -ae — 'fidei' ends in -ei
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension nouns have genitive in -is — 'fidei' ends in -ei
hints.4th declension: 4th declension nouns have genitive in -ūs — 'fidei' ends in -ei

hints.Nominative case: No preposition governs the nominative — 'ex' takes the ablative
hints.Genitive case: Prepositions like 'causa' take genitive — 'ex' takes the ablative
hints.Accusative case: Prepositions like 'ad' and 'in' (motion) take accusative — 'ex' takes the ablative

translation: The just one lives by faith
